subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 12! In this unit you will learn how to collect data, organise it into tables, and display it clearly using bar charts, dot plots, waffle diagrams, frequency diagrams and line graphs. You will also learn the harder and more interesting skill of interpreting data – reading a chart, spotting patterns, and drawing sensible conclusions from what the numbers tell you.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 5 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Collect data systematically and record it neatly in a frequency table using tally marks.
Draw and read bar charts, choosing a sensible scale and labelling both axes and the title.
Draw and read dot plots, and use them to find the mode, range, clusters and gaps.
Draw and read waffle diagrams to show proportions, and convert between fractions, decimals and percentages.
Draw and read frequency diagrams for grouped, continuous data such as times and heights.
Draw and read line graphs that show how a quantity changes over time, and describe the trend.
Choose the most suitable display for a given set of data, and explain your choice.
Calculate the mode, range and mean (average) of a small set of numbers.
Interpret data carefully, ask good questions, and spot when a chart is misleading.

concepts.title: Collecting and Organising Data
concepts.explanation: Before you can draw any chart, you must collect your data and organise it. Raw data – the answers exactly as people give them – usually arrives as a messy jumble. Suppose Khadija asks 20 classmates how they travel to school and writes down each answer as she hears it: walk, car, walk, bus, walk, bike, car, walk, bus, walk, car, walk, bus, walk, bike, car, walk, bus, walk, car. It is very hard to see anything in that jumble. The cure is a frequency table. A frequency table has a row for each category and a column…
concepts.example: Here is Khadija’s data organised. Walk appears 9 times, Car appears 5 times, Bus appears 4 times, and Bike appears 2 times. Now the story is clear at a glance: walking is by far the most common way to reach school.…

concepts.title: Bar Charts
concepts.explanation: A bar chart is the best display for comparing separate categories, such as favourite sports or types of pet. Each bar stands for one category. The height of the bar shows the frequency – the taller the bar, the more items in that category. Because the categories are separate, the bars are drawn the same width with small gaps between them. Let us build one together. Suppose a survey of Farah’s class gives these favourite pets: Dogs 12, Cats 8, Fish 4, Birds 3, Hamsters 3. Here is how to draw the chart step by step:
concepts.example: Organise the data into a frequency table first, so you know your largest value (here it is 12).

concepts.title: Dot Plots, Mode and Range
concepts.explanation: A dot plot shows data by stacking dots above a number line. Each dot stands for one piece of data. Dot plots are perfect for numerical data where you want to see every individual value and how the values are spread out. Suppose the ages of children visiting a zoo in Nairobi are: 3, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6, 6, 7, 8, 8, 9, 10. To draw the dot plot you first draw a number line covering every value from the smallest (3) to the largest (10): 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10. Then, for each piece of data, you place one dot above the…
concepts.example: Dot plots make three important features easy to see. The mode is the value with the tallest stack of dots – the most common value. In the zoo data, age 5 has three dots, more than any other, so the mode is 5. The range…

concepts.title: Waffle Diagrams and Proportions
concepts.explanation: A waffle diagram is a grid of squares that shows proportions – how a whole is divided into parts. It works like a pie chart made from squares instead of slices. When the grid has exactly 100 squares (a 10 by 10 grid), reading percentages becomes wonderfully easy, because each square is worth exactly 1%. Suppose 100 pupils are surveyed about how they travel to school: Walk 35, Car 28, Bus 25, Bike 8, Other 4. To make the waffle diagram you draw a 10 by 10 grid, choose a different colour for each category, and…
concepts.example: Waffle diagrams are a natural place to practise moving between fractions, decimals and percentages, because a proportion can be written in all three ways. If 8 pupils out of 100 cycle, that proportion is the fraction…

concepts.title: Frequency Diagrams for Continuous Data
concepts.explanation: Some data is continuous, which means it is measured rather than counted and can take any value in a range – for example times, heights, weights and temperatures. Idris did not finish the school race in exactly 8 minutes; he finished in 8 minutes and a few seconds. To display continuous data we sort it into groups (also called intervals) and draw a frequency diagram. A frequency diagram looks like a bar chart, but with two key differences. First, the x-axis shows ranges such as “0–5 minutes”, “5–10 minutes” and…
concepts.example: Choosing sensible groups is a skill in itself. You want enough groups to show the shape of the data, but not so many that most groups are nearly empty. Equal-width groups (all 5 minutes wide here) keep the diagram fair,…

concepts.title: Line Graphs and Trends
concepts.explanation: A line graph shows how a quantity changes over time. Time always runs along the horizontal x-axis (hours, days, weeks, months), and the quantity being measured runs up the vertical y-axis. You plot a point for each reading and then join the points with straight lines. Line graphs are the right choice whenever your question is about change – temperature through the day, a plant’s growth over weeks, or money raised over several weeks of a fundraiser. Here is the method. First set up the axes, with time on the x-axis…
concepts.example: Consider a week of midday temperatures in Mombasa: Monday 22°C, Tuesday 25°C, Wednesday 28°C, Thursday 26°C, Friday 23°C, Saturday 20°C, Sunday 18°C. Plotted as a line graph, the line climbs to a peak on Wednesday and…

explorations.title: Line Graphs and Trends
explorations.context: A line graph shows how a quantity changes over time. Time always runs along the horizontal x-axis (hours, days, weeks, months), and the quantity being measured runs up the vertical y-axis. You plot a point for each reading and then join the points with…
explorations.prompt: Rainfall over a week (mm): 12, 8, 15, 3, 18, 22, 9. a) Find the total. b) Find the mean. c) Which day type had more rain – imagine Mon–Fri are the first five values and Sat–Sun the last two.
explorations.answer: 1) a) 12 + 8 + 15 + 3 + 18 + 22 + 9 = 87 mm; b) mean = 87 ÷ 7 ≈ 12.4 mm; c) Mon–Fri = 12 + 8 + 15 + 3 + 18 = 56 mm vs Sat–Sun = 22 + 9 = 31 mm, so the first five days had more.
explorations.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

methods.title: How to draw a bar chart
methods.example: Amina asks 40 pupils their favourite sport and records: Soccer 15, Basketball 12, Tennis 8, Swimming 5. Make a frequency table and check the total.
steps: Organise the data in a frequency table and find the largest value.
Choose a scale that goes up in equal steps and starts at zero.
Draw and label both axes and give the chart a title.
Draw equal-width bars with equal gaps, each as tall as its frequency.

methods.title: How to find the mean
methods.example: Using the sports data above, what scale should the y-axis use, and which sport gives the tallest bar?
steps: Add together all the values to get the total.
Count how many values there are.
Divide the total by the number of values.
Round sensibly if asked (for example, to one decimal place).

methods.title: How to read a line graph
methods.example: What percentage of the 40 pupils chose Basketball?
steps: Read the axes so you know what time and quantity are shown.
Describe the overall trend: rising, falling, or level.
Find any sudden change (a sharp rise or fall) or repeating pattern.
Make a careful prediction, remembering it is only an estimate.

methods.title: Mode and range from a dot plot
methods.example: The number of books read last month by 10 pupils is: 2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3, 3, 5, 2, 1. Find the mode and the range.
steps: Solution: Count each value: 1 appears twice, 2 appears three times, 3 appears three times, 4 once, 5 once.
The most common values are 2 and 3 (three times each), so the data has two modes, 2 and 3.
The range is the highest minus the lowest = 5 − 1 = 4.

methods.title: Calculate the mean
methods.example: Find the mean of the weekly temperatures 18, 22, 25, 23, 20, 17 and 15 °C.
steps: There are 7 values, so the mean = 140 ÷ 7 = 20°C.
On average the week was 20°C.

methods.title: Describe a trend
methods.example: A line graph of the temperatures in Example 5 (Monday to Sunday) rises to Wednesday then falls. Describe the trend and predict the next Monday.
steps: Solution: The temperature rose from Monday (18°C) to a peak on Wednesday (25°C), then fell steadily to Sunday (15°C).
Following the falling trend of about 2–3°C per day, next Monday might be around 12–13°C – but this is only an estimate, as the weather may change.
Check the describe a trend result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Build a frequency table
workedExamples.prompt: Amina asks 40 pupils their favourite sport and records: Soccer 15, Basketball 12, Tennis 8, Swimming 5. Make a frequency table and check the total.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: List each sport with its frequency: Soccer 15, Basketball 12, Tennis 8, Swimming 5. Add the frequencies: 15 + 12 + 8 + 5 = 40, which matches the number of pupils asked, so the table is complete and correct.

workedExamples.title: Choose a scale for a bar chart
workedExamples.prompt: Using the sports data above, what scale should the y-axis use, and which sport gives the tallest bar?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The largest frequency is 15, so a scale going up in 2s (0, 2, 4 … 16) is sensible and easy to read. Soccer gives the tallest bar because 15 is the largest frequency.

workedExamples.title: Find the percentage
workedExamples.prompt: What percentage of the 40 pupils chose Basketball?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use (part ÷ whole) × 100 = (12 ÷ 40) × 100 = 0.30 × 100 = 30%. So 30% of the pupils chose basketball.

workedExamples.title: Mode and range from a dot plot
workedExamples.prompt: The number of books read last month by 10 pupils is: 2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3, 3, 5, 2, 1. Find the mode and the range.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count each value: 1 appears twice, 2 appears three times, 3 appears three times, 4 once, 5 once. The most common values are 2 and 3 (three times each), so the data has two modes, 2 and 3. The range is the highest minus the lowest = 5 − 1 = 4.

workedExamples.title: Calculate the mean
workedExamples.prompt: Find the mean of the weekly temperatures 18, 22, 25, 23, 20, 17 and 15 °C.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add them: 18 + 22 + 25 + 23 + 20 + 17 + 15 = 140. There are 7 values, so the mean = 140 ÷ 7 = 20°C. On average the week was 20°C.

workedExamples.title: Describe a trend
workedExamples.prompt: A line graph of the temperatures in Example 5 (Monday to Sunday) rises to Wednesday then falls. Describe the trend and predict the next Monday.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The temperature rose from Monday (18°C) to a peak on Wednesday (25°C), then fell steadily to Sunday (15°C). Following the falling trend of about 2–3°C per day, next Monday might be around 12–13°C – but this is only an estimate, as the weather may change.

workedExamples.title: Waffle diagram proportions
workedExamples.prompt: In a 100-square waffle diagram, 8 squares are coloured for pupils who cycle. Write this proportion as a fraction in simplest form, a decimal and a percentage.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: As a fraction it is 8/100, which simplifies to 2/25 (dividing top and bottom by 4). As a decimal it is 0.08. As a percentage it is 8%, because each of the 100 squares is worth 1%.

workedExamples.title: Compare two data sets
workedExamples.prompt: Class A scores have a mean of 82.9 and a range of 15. Class B scores have a mean of 89.5 and a range of 8. Which class did better, and which was more consistent?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Class B did better on average, because its mean (89.5) is higher than Class A’s (82.9). Class B was also more consistent, because its range (8) is smaller than Class A’s (15), so its scores are bunched more closely together.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Say whether each is discrete or continuous data: a) number of goats; b) the time to run a race; c) favourite fruit; d) the height of a plant.
workedExamples.solution: a) mode = 5; b) range = 8 − 3 = 5; c) there is a gap at 7 (no data).

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: For the data 20, 24, 22, 26, 18, find the mean and the range.
workedExamples.solution: a) discrete; b) continuous; c) discrete; d) continuous.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: Would a bar chart, line graph or waffle diagram be best for: a) how a baby’s weight changes over months; b) the fraction of a class choosing each lunch option; c) comparing votes for four class captains?

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: Class A scores: 78, 82, 85, 79, 88, 91, 76, 83, 87, 80. Find the mean and the range. Show your working.

activities.title: Class (or Family) Data Detective
activities.materials: paper, coloured pencils, a ruler, squared paper.
steps: Choose one survey question, such as ‘What is your favourite fruit – mango, banana, papaya or dates?’ or ‘How do you travel to school?’
Survey 20 people (family, friends, neighbours) and record each answer with a tally mark.
Turn your tallies into a frequency table and check the totals add to 20.
Draw the same data three ways: a bar chart, a dot plot, and a waffle diagram (use a 4 by 5 grid of 20 squares).
Decide which chart shows the information most clearly, and write one sentence explaining why.

activities.title: Weather Station Project
activities.materials: a thermometer or a weather app, paper, squared paper, coloured pencils.
steps: Record the temperature at the same time each day for 7 days (for example, just after Dhuhr prayer).
Write each reading in a table with columns for the day, the temperature, and a short weather description.
Draw a line graph of the temperatures, plotting each point carefully with a ruler.
Describe the trend in one sentence, and mark the day with the biggest change from the day before.
Predict the next day’s temperature from your trend, then check how close you were.

activities.title: Waffle Diagram Colour Challenge
activities.materials: squared paper marked into a 10 by 10 grid (100 squares), coloured pencils.
steps: Survey 100 items you can find or imagine – for example the colours of 100 cars passing, or 100 answers to a favourite-food question.
Colour the grid so each category fills the right number of squares (each square is 1%).
Write the percentage beside each colour in a small key.
For one category, write its proportion as a fraction, a decimal and a percentage.
Challenge a family member to read your diagram and tell you which category is the largest.

activities.title: The Averages Dice Game
activities.materials: one dice (or numbers 1–6 on folded paper), a pencil and paper.
steps: Roll the dice ten times and write down every number you roll.
Find the mode (the number rolled most often) and the range (highest roll minus lowest).
Add all ten numbers and divide by 10 to find the mean.
Play again and see whether the mode, range and mean change. Talk about why they do.
Challenge: predict what the mean will be over many rolls, and test your idea with 30 rolls.

activities.title: Sports Statistics Analyst (Investigation)
activities.materials: paper, squared paper, coloured pencils, and scores from a favourite team.
steps: Collect the goals or points your chosen team scored in their last 8 games.
Draw a line graph of the scores game by game and describe whether the team is improving or not.
Work out the team’s mean score per game and its range.
Draw a bar chart comparing goals scored with goals conceded.
Write three findings about the team’s performance and one recommendation for the coach.

activities.title: Spot the Misleading Chart (Family Task)
activities.materials: newspapers, magazines, or charts you have seen online or on a phone; paper and pencil.
steps: With a family member, find or sketch a chart from the news or an advertisement.
Check three things: Does the scale start at zero? Are the axes labelled? Is it clear how the data was collected?
Decide together whether the chart is honest or misleading, and explain your reasons.
Redraw one misleading chart fairly, with an even scale and clear labels.
Talk about why honest charts matter when we make decisions or spend money.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: The most important decision is the scale. If your scale steps are too large – say, going up in 10s when your biggest value is 12 – all the bars look almost the same height and the differences vanish. If the steps are too small, the chart becomes impossibly tall. A good rule is to look at your largest value and pick a step size so the tallest bar uses most of the height of the page. The scale must also go up in equal steps and normally start at zero; a scale that starts partway up is one of the commonest ways charts are made misleading, as you will see in Concept 8.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: Waffle diagrams are a natural place to practise moving between fractions, decimals and percentages, because a proportion can be written in all three ways. If 8 pupils out of 100 cycle, that proportion is the fraction 8/100, the decimal 0.08, and the percentage 8%. The fraction 8/100 simplifies to 2/25. To find a percentage from any total – not just 100 – you use the rule (part ÷ whole) × 100. For instance, if 5 pupils out of 20 chose the daisy, that is (5 ÷ 20) × 100 = 25%.

terms: How many times a value or category occurs
The size of each equal step on an axis
Measured data (times, heights) with no gaps
Counted data in separate whole amounts
The overall direction data is moving over time
A part compared with the whole
commonMistakes: Always add a title and label both axes
Start at zero and use equal steps
Make the bars touch for grouped data
Mode = most common; mean = total shared out

realProblems.prompt: A well-charity in Garissa raises shillings over five weeks: 245, 312, 189, 398, 276. a) Find the total raised. b) Find the mean per week. c) If the goal is 2000, how much more is needed?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: 100 pupils are surveyed on transport: Walk 35, Car 28, Bus 25, Bike 8, Other 4. a) On a waffle diagram how many squares are walking? b) What percentage use a motor vehicle (car + bus)? c) In a school of 400, estimate how many walk.
realProblems.answer: a) 35 squares; b) car + bus = 28 + 25 = 53%; c) 35% of 400 = 140 pupils.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: a) 35 squares; b) car + bus = 28 + 25 = 53%; c) 35% of 400 = 140 pupils.

realProblems.prompt: Two market stalls record daily sales of sambusas over four days. Stall 1: 30, 45, 25, 40. Stall 2: 38, 34, 36, 32. Find the mean for each stall, then say which stall’s sales are more consistent and how you know.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A line graph shows a plant’s height each week: 2 cm, 5 cm, 9 cm, 12 cm, 14 cm. a) Describe the trend. b) By how much did it grow between week 2 and week 3? c) Predict week 6’s height and say why it is only an estimate.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Aisha surveys 20 families about the number of children: 2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3, 3, 5, 2, 4, 1, 3, 2, 6, 3, 4, 2, 3, 2, 4. Find the mode, the range and the mean (to one decimal place).
realProblems.answer: ÷ 4 = 140 ÷ 4 = 35; both average 35, but Stall 2 is more consistent (range
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: ÷ 4 = 140 ÷ 4 = 35; both average 35, but Stall 2 is more consistent (range

realProblems.prompt: Class A scores: 78, 82, 85, 79, 88, 91, 76, 83, 87, 80. Find the mean and the range. Show your working.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Class A scores: 78, 82, 85, 79, 88, 91, 76, 83, 87, 80. Find the mean and the range. Show your working.
Class B scores: 85, 89, 92, 87, 94, 88, 90, 86, 91, 93. Find the mean and the range, then say which class is more consistent and why.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Total = 895, mean = 89.5; range = 94 − 86 = 8; Class B is more consistent because its range (8) is smaller than Class A’s (15).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A bar chart’s y-axis starts at 70 instead of 0. Explain, with an example, how this makes the differences between scores look bigger than they are.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: If two scores are 78 and 82, on a 0-start axis the bars are almost the same height, but on a 70-start axis one bar is twice as tall as the other, so a small 4-mark difference looks huge.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In a survey of 20 pupils about books read, the results are 2, 3, 1, 4, 2, 3, 3, 5, 2, 1, 4, 3, 2, 6, 3, 4, 1, 3, 2, 4. Build a frequency table, then find the mode and the range.
Explain the key idea in Frequency Diagrams for Continuous Data.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Some data is continuous, which means it is measured rather than counted and can take any value in a range – for example times, heights, weights and temperatures. Idris did not finish the school race in exactly 8 minutes; he finished in 8 minutes and a few seconds. To display continuous data we sort it into groups (also called intervals) and draw a frequency diagram. A frequency diagram looks like a bar chart, but with two key differences. First, the x-axis shows ranges such as “0–5 minutes”, “5–10 minutes” and…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Line Graphs and Trends.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A line graph shows how a quantity changes over time. Time always runs along the horizontal x-axis (hours, days, weeks, months), and the quantity being measured runs up the vertical y-axis. You plot a point for each reading and then join the points with straight lines. Line graphs are the right choice whenever your question is about change – temperature through the day, a plant’s growth over weeks, or money raised over several weeks of a fundraiser. Here is the method. First set up the axes, with time on the x-axis…

options: How many times a value or category occurs

options: How many times a value or category occurs
questions.answer: How many times a value or category occurs

questions.explanation: Frequency means How many times a value or category occurs.

questions.question: What does “Mode” mean?
options: The size of each equal step on an axis
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Mode means The most common value.

questions.question: What does “Range” mean?
options: The size of each equal step on an axis
Measured data (times, heights) with no gaps
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Range means Highest value minus lowest value.

questions.question: What does “Mean” mean?
options: The size of each equal step on an axis
Measured data (times, heights) with no gaps
Counted data in separate whole amounts
questions.hint: Use the Unit 12 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Mean means The total shared equally (the average).

questions.question: What does “Scale” mean?
options: The size of each equal step on an axis
Measured data (times, heights) with no gaps
Counted data in separate whole amounts
The overall direction data is moving over time

selfAssessment: I can collect data systematically and record it neatly in a frequency table using tally marks.
I can draw and read bar charts, choosing a sensible scale and labelling both axes and the title.
I can draw and read dot plots, and use them to find the mode, range, clusters and gaps.
I can draw and read waffle diagrams to show proportions, and convert between fractions, decimals and percentages.
I can draw and read frequency diagrams for grouped, continuous data such as times and heights.
I can draw and read line graphs that show how a quantity changes over time, and describe the trend.
I can choose the most suitable display for a given set of data, and explain your choice.
I can calculate the mode, range and mean (average) of a small set of numbers.
